coredata.dc:title: An aptasensor for chloramphenicol determination based on dual signal output of photoelectrochemistry and colorimetry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xingyang
Wei, Haiyue
Tian, Jiuying
Lu, Jusheng
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, we developed an aptasensor to determine chloramphenicol (CAP) based on the dual signal output of photoelectrochemistry (PEC) and colorimetry. The Fe3+-doped porous tungsten trioxide was prepared by sol-gel method and coated on the ITO conductive glass to form ITO/p-W(Fe)O3. After assembling the captured DNA (cDNA) and the aptamer of CAP (apt) successively, the constructed ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/apt aptasensor exhibited excellent photocurrent response under visible light irradiation in the presence of glucose, which provided the feasibility for PEC measurement with high sensitivity. In the presence of CAP, the apt left the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 surface and AuNPs linked on the probe DNA would be assembled on it, which led to the decrease of photocurrent. Thanks to the oxidase-mimic catalytic performance of AuNPs and the recycling catalytic hydrolysis by exonuclease I, the measurement signal of the aptasensor could be amplified significantly, and the photocurrent decrease of the aptasensor was linearly related to the concentration of CAP in the range of 1.0 pM–10.0 nM and low detection limit was 0.36 pM. Meanwhile, the H2O2 produced from catalytic oxidation of glucose could oxidize TMB to blue oxTMB under HRP catalysis, which absorbance at 652 nm was linearly related to the concentration of CAP in the range of 5.0 pM–10.0 nM and low detection limit was 1.72 pM. Therefore, an aptasensor that determine CAP in real samples was successfully constructed with good precision of the relative standard deviation less than 5.7 % for PEC method and 7.3 % for colorimetric method, which can meet the analysis needs in different scenarios.
coredata.dcterms:subject: p-W(Fe)O3
AuNP nanozyme
PEC and colorimetric aptasensor
Catalytic oxidation of glucose
Chloramphenicol determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 and construction of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/apt aptasensor
PEC or colorimetric aptasensing for CAP in real samples
Preparation and characterization of ITO/p-WO3 doped with Fe3+
Electrochemical, PEC and optical performances of ITO/p-WO3 doped with different amount of ferric ions
Catalytic performance of AuNPs for glucose oxidation
PEC performances of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 under different conditions
Construction of the PEC or colorimetric aptasensor for CAP determination
Analytical performances of the aptasensor for CAP determination

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Top-notch Academic Programs Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (TAPP), the Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation for Higher Education Institutions (19KJB150026), and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Normal University (2024XKT0557, 2022XKT0305) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of ITO/p-WO3 doped with Fe3+. (B) XRD patterns of ITO, p-WO3 and ITO/p-WO3 doped with Fe3+. (C) XPS survey spectrum of ferric-doped p-WO3. (D) High resolution XPS spectrum of Fe 2p. Polarization curves (E) and photocurrent response (F) of p-WO3 doped with different amount of ferric ions under visible-light irradiation. (G) Nyquist plots of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of p-WO3 doped with different amount of ferric ions in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 5.0 mM Fe(CN)6 4−/3- and 0.1 M KCl. UV–vis absorption spectra (H) and corresponding Tauc's plots (I) of p-WO3 doped with different amount of ferric ions. Doped with (a) 0, (b) 0.2 %, (c) 0.5 %, or (d) 1.0 % ferric ions.

TEM of Au NPs (A) and pDNA-Au (E). (B) Photograph of TMB solution after adding of AuNPs for different time (0–10 min). (C) UV–vis spectra of TMB after adding Au NPs in 0–10 mM glucose solution (a–f) and then adding 1.0 μM HRP. Michaelis-Menten curves and corresponding Lineweaver-Burk plot of AuNPs (D) or pDNA-Au (F) for glucose catalytic oxidation. Wavelength for measuring absorbance: 652 nm.

(A) Photocurrent of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 in anaerobic solution, aerobic solution, anaerobic solution containing 10 mM glucose, or aerobic solution containing 10 mM glucose under visible-light irradiation, respectively. (B) Photocurrent of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 in anaerobic solution, ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA in anaerobic solution, ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/pDNA-Au in anaerobic solution or aerobic solution. (C) Photocurrent change of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/pDNA-Au aerobic solution containing 10 mM glucose with increasing irradiation time. (D) UV–vis spectra of (C solution) after 2 min of irradiation with adding 0.1 mM TMB and 1.0 μM HRP.

(A) Photocurrent of the aptasensor for different concentration of CAP (a–l: 0, 0.001, 0.002, 0.005, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0 nM). (B) Calibration curve of lg△I versus lgC CAP in the range of 0.001–10.0 nM (0.001, 0.002, 0.005, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0 nM). (C) UV–vis spectra of the corresponding PEC-analyzed solutions in A (a–k: 0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0 nM) with adding 0.1 mM TMB and 1.0 μM HRP. (D) Calibration curve of△A versus lgC CAP in the range of 0.005–10.0 nM (0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0 nM). (E) Selectivity of the aptasensor by PEC method for 50 nM of thiamphenicol (TAP), florfenicol (FF), kanamycin (KAN) or streptomycin sulphate (SS), or 1.0 nM CAP. (F) Stability testing of the aptasensor by PEC method.
The mechanism of determining CAP of the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/apt aptasensor.
Comparison of the present assay for the determination of CAP with previous reports.
Determination of CAP with the present aptasensor (n = 6).

head.ce:title: An aptasensor for chloramphenicol determination based on dual signal output of photoelectrochemistry and colorimetry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Green Synthetic Chemistry for Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jiangsu Normal University, Xuzhou, 221116, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Green Synthetic Chemistry for Functional Materials
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Green Synthetic Chemistry for Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jiangsu Normal University, Xuzhou, 221116, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jiangsu Normal University, 101 Shanghai Road, Xuzhou, 221116, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, we developed an aptasensor to determine chloramphenicol (CAP) based on the dual signal output of photoelectrochemistry (PEC) and colorimetry. The Fe3+-doped porous tungsten trioxide was prepared by sol-gel method and coated on the ITO conductive glass to form ITO/p-W(Fe)O3. After assembling the captured DNA (cDNA) and the aptamer of CAP (apt) successively, the constructed ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/apt aptasensor exhibited excellent photocurrent response under visible light irradiation in the presence of glucose, which provided the feasibility for PEC measurement with high sensitivity. In the presence of CAP, the apt left the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 surface and AuNPs linked on the probe DNA would be assembled on it, which led to the decrease of photocurrent. Thanks to the oxidase-mimic catalytic performance of AuNPs and the recycling catalytic hydrolysis by exonuclease I, the measurement signal of the aptasensor could be amplified significantly, and the photocurrent decrease of the aptasensor was linearly related to the concentration of CAP in the range of 1.0 pM–10.0 nM and low detection limit was 0.36 pM. Meanwhile, the H2O2 produced from catalytic oxidation of glucose could oxidize TMB to blue oxTMB under HRP catalysis, which absorbance at 652 nm was linearly related to the concentration of CAP in the range of 5.0 pM–10.0 nM and low detection limit was 1.72 pM. Therefore, an aptasensor that determine CAP in real samples was successfully constructed with good precision of the relative standard deviation less than 5.7 % for PEC method and 7.3 % for colorimetric method, which can meet the analysis needs in different scenarios.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chloramphenicol (CAP) is a broad-spectrum antibiotic with inhibitory effect on pathogenic Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial growth [1]. However, CAP is highly toxic to humans which inhibits bone marrow hematopoietic function and causes aplastic anemia [2]. Long term consumption of animal food containing CAP residual may cause dysbiosis of gut microbiota and formation of inhibiting antibodies [3]. Therefore, China and America have explicitly prohibited the use of CAP in all animal-based foods [4], and it is particularly necessary to determine the CAP concentration quickly and sensitively in real samples.

At present, several methods have been developed for CAP determination, such as high-performance liquid chromatography [5], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [6] and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [7]. However, these methods require sophisticated instruments, complex operations, and time-consuming sample pretreatment, which may not be suitable for rapid detection. Some other methods, such as electrochemistry [8,9], fluorescence [10], colorimetry [11], and photoelectrochemistry (PEC) techniques [12] were also developed to determine CAP.

Because of the complete separation of photocurrent signal from excitation light, the PEC technique can provide a low background noise for the biosensors, and has received widespread attention due to its combination of optical and electrochemical advantages [13–16]. To construct the PEC biosensor, it is particularly important to select a suitable photoactive nanomaterial. In general, single photosensitive material was rarely used for PEC analysis in previous reports due to the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [17–20]. For example, tungsten oxide (WO3) can show excellent visible-light response due to its narrow band gap energy [21]. However, the PEC performance of WO3 is relatively poor due to high recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, similar to most single-phase metal oxide semiconductors [22,23]. Currently, the main method to improve the PEC activity of WO3 is to form heterojunctions with other photosensitive materials [24–26]. However, there have been no reports on improving its PEC performance through doping means.

Herein, we first prepared porous tungsten trioxide nanomaterials on the indium tin oxide (ITO) glass using sol-gel method and doped with 0.5 % of Fe3+ to form ITO/p-W(Fe)O3. Then, as shown in Scheme 1 , the captured DNA (cDNA) was assembled on the surface of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 to form ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA. After binding to the aptamer of CAP (apt), cDNA and apt would form a double stranded rigid structure to construct an ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/apt aptasensor, which exhibited excellent photocurrent response under visible light irradiation in the presence of glucose, enabling the constructed aptasensor with a relatively high sensitivity for PEC measurement. In the presence of CAP, the apt would leave the ITO surface due to its specific binding with CAP, the probe DNA linked with AuNPs (pDNA-Au) would hybridize with cDNA on the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3, and the AuNPs would be assembled on the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3. Due to the oxidase-mimic performance of AuNPs for glucose oxidation, the photocurrent would decrease. With the assistance of the recycling catalytic hydrolysis by exonuclease I (Exo I), a kind of enzyme that specifically cleaves a single-stranded DNA in the 3′–5′ direction [27], more AuNPs would be assembled on the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3, the photocurrent would decrease significantly due to the oxidase-mimic performance of AuNPs for glucose oxidation, and the sensitivity of the aptasensor for PEC determining CAP would be improved. Meanwhile, AuNPs catalyzed the oxidation of glucose to produce hydrogen peroxide, which could oxidize 3,3′,5,5′- tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxTMB under the catalysis of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), so the concentration of CAP could also be determined by colorimetry method with the advantages of simple and fast operation. Therefore, an aptasensor that could simultaneously determine CAP through both colorimetric and PEC methods would be successfully constructed to meet the analysis requirements in different scenarios.

CAP, chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), ferric nitrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), ammonium metatungstate ((NH4)6H2W12O40), HRP, TMB and Exo I were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Citric acid, styrene, potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and carbonyl diimidazole were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The captured DNA (cDNA), aptamer of CAP (apt), and probed DNA with hairpin structure (pDNA) were synthesized by Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and their sequences were listed as follow. cDNA: 3′-ACGTA GTGTC TCCAG TCTAC TGAAG TCACT CAACA GGGTG-5′; pDNA: 5′-TGCAT CACAG AGGTC AGATG ACGCA–SH–3′; apt: 5′-AGCAG CACAG AGGTC AGATG ACTTC AGTGA GTTGT CCCAC GGTCG GCGAG TCGGT GGTAG CCTAT GCGTG CTACC GTGAA-3′. Deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm) was prepared by Milli-Q water purification system. And the main apparatuses could be found in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 and construction of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/apt aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Before preparing ITO/p-W(Fe)O3, the polystyrene latex was first prepared. Specifically, 2.5 mL of styrene was mixed with 10 mL of water under stirring, and heated to 70 °C in N2 atmosphere for 10 min, 1.0 mL of 5.0 mM K2S2O8 solution was added, and raised the temperature to 90 °C immediately, continued under stirring for 3 h to form the polystyrene latex. Then, 1.3 g of ammonium metatungstate, 0.3 g of citric acid, and 10.0 mg of ferric nitrate were added into 5.0 mL of water and heated at 70 °C to disperse evenly. Follow by, 20 μL of the above mixed solution and 80 μL of the prepared polystyrene latex were added into 200 μL water, stirred for 0.5 h and dripped onto the ITO glass, dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 3 h and transferred to a muffle furnace heated to 400 °C for 2 h to obtain ITO/p-W(Fe)O3.

The cDNA was assembled on the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 to form ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA through amide bonds using carbonyl diimidazole as an activator [28,29]. In details, the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 was firstly immersed 1.0 mL of dried DMSO containing 1.0 mg carbonyl diimidazole, and mildly vibrated at room temperature for 30 min. Then, the activated ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 was immersed in a 1.0 mL phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M pH 7.4) containing 2.0 μM cDNA, kept at room temperature for 1 h to obtain the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA. Follow by, the prepared ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA was immersed in 1.0 mL PBS solution (0.1 M pH 7.4) containing 2.0 μM apt and kept for 30 min to construct an ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/apt aptasensor.

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were firstly prepared by reducing HAuCl4 with trisodium citrate. Briefly, 2.0 mL of 10.0 mM trisodium citrate was added rapidly into a boiling aqueous solution containing 10 mL of 0.5 mM HAuCl4, kept boiling and stirred for 15 min to form the final colloidal AuNP solution. Then, 1.0 mL PBS solution (0.1 M pH 7.4) containing 5.0 μM thiol-pDNA was added into 1.0 mL of the above prepared colloidal AuNP solution, stirred for 30 min to form pDNA-Au. To remove excess pDNA, the solution was centrifuged at 13,200 rpm for 10 min, then redispersed in 1.0 mL of 0.1 M pH 7.4 PBS solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC or colorimetric aptasensing for CAP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, the lake water and milk samples were selected to investigate the practical application of the proposed method in the fields of environment and food safety. The lake water was taken from the Yuquan River on the campus of Jiangsu Normal University and directly used after filtration with a 0.22 μm filter. The milk samples were purchased in the local supermarket (Xuzhou, China). Firstly, 2.0 mL of 3 % (w/v) trichloroacetic acid aqueous solution was added into 20 mL of milk sample and stirred vigorously for 30 min, then 2 mL of methanol was added and continue stirring for 10 min, centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter for subsequent CAP determination.

Before determining CAP, the constructed ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/apt aptasensor was firstly immersed into 5.0 mL PBS solution (0.1 M, pH 7.4) containing 10 mM glucose, measured the photocurrent value under visible light irradiation and recorded it as I
0 . Then, 100 μL of the above sample supernatant was added into 5.0 mL PBS solution (0.1 M, pH 7.4) containing 1.0 μM pDNA-Au and 50 U Exo I, and the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/apt was immersed in which, stirred and kept for 10 min, measured the photocurrent value under visible light irradiation and recorded it as I. Thus, the concentration of CAP in real sample was determined by PEC technique according to the photocurrent change value (△I
I
0 – I). In addition, the CAP concentration could also be determined by the colorimetric method. In details, 2.0 mL of the above PEC-analyzed solution was mixed with 10 μL of 1.0 μM HRP and 20 μL of 0.1 mM TMB, and measured its absorbance (A) after 5 min. At the same time, the absorbance of the blank solution (A
0 ) was measured, so the concentration of CAP in real sample was determined by colorimetric method based on the absorbance change (△A = A - A
0 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of ITO/p-WO3 doped with Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, we firstly prepared polystyrene and used it as template to synthesize pore WO3 (p-WO3) doped with ferric ions on the ITO. When the amount of styrene and K2S2O8 initiator was 20.0 mmol and 5.0 μmol respectively, the particle size of the synthesized polystyrene was about 200 nm (Fig. S1). Using such polystyrene as the template, the synthesized ferric-doped p-WO3 on ITO exhibited a uniform pore structure distribution, which pore size was similar to that of polystyrene (Fig. 1 A). Comparatively, the structure of ferric-doped WO3 synthesized on ITO without polystyrene as a template was dense (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. 1B, pure p-WO3 exhibited characteristic X-ray diffraction (XRD) peaks at 23.1°, 23.6°, and 24.4°, which belonged to the (002), (020), and (200) crystal planes of WO3 triclinic crystal structure, respectively (PDF#20–1323). Compared with the XRD patterns of ITO and p-WO3 (curve a and b of Fig. 1B), no characteristic peak of ferric ion was observed in the XRD pattern of ferric-doped p-WO3 (curve c of Fig. 1B), indicating only a very low content of ferric ion-doping. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) could illustrate the presence of small amount ferric ions in p-WO3 (Fig. 1C), the two peaks at 724.2 eV and 710.7 eV in the high resolution XPS of Fe 2p (Fig. 1D) were corresponding to Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 asymmetric bands derived from the spin-orbital splitting [30], indicating the successful doping of ferric ions in p-WO3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical, PEC and optical performances of ITO/p-WO3 doped with different amount of ferric ions
ce:section.ce:para: The results in Fig. S3A showed that, the prepared porous WO3 (p-WO3) on ITO using polystyrene as a template had a PEC response under visible light irradiation, and the photocurrent of p-WO3 increased with the positive shift of bias potential, exhibiting a significant anodic photocurrent, which was significantly higher than that of WO3 prepared without polystyrene template (Fig. S3B). This should be because the porous structure of p-WO3 is conducive to light absorption. However, due to the recombination of photogenerated electrons-holes, the photocurrent response of p-WO3 was not very high, which was only 15 μA at a bias potential of +0.4 V (Fig. S3B). Therefore, to suppress the recombination of photogenerated electrons-holes and further improve the PEC performance of p-WO3, we doped a certain amount of ferric ions into p-WO3. The experimental results indicated that (Fig. 1E and F), the doping of ferric ions had a certain improvement on the PEC performance of p-WO3, which photocurrent values were significantly higher that those of previous report [26]. As the doped amount of ferric ions increased, the photocurrent of ITO/p-WO3 increased, and reached its maximum when the doping amount of ferric ions was 0.5 %. Continuing to doping ferric ion instead decreased the photocurrent. It may be due to the excessive amount of ferric ions being converted into Fe2O3 during high-temperature calcination, which hindered electron transfer (Fig. 1G). The reason why ferric-doping could effectively improve the PEC performance of p-WO3 mainly included the following aspects. Firstly, ferric-doping could effectively improve the electron-transfer performance of p-WO3 and suppressed the recombination of photogenerated electrons-holes (Fig. 1G). Secondly, from the UV–vis absorption spectra of ferric-doped p-WO3 and the corresponding Tauc's plots (Fig. 1H and I), the doping of ferric ions decreased the band gap energy (Eg) of p-WO3, which significantly enhanced the absorption of visible light and improved its photocurrent response. Among them, when the doping amount of ferric ions was 0.5 %, Eg of ferric-doped p-WO3 was 2.55 eV and was significantly lower than that of pure p-WO3 (Fig. 1I), which was more conducive to the absorption of visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic performance of AuNPs for glucose oxidation
ce:section.ce:para:
In the present work, the synthesized AuNPs had good dispersibility with a particle size of 6–7 nm (Fig. 2 A). Like most nanozymes [31], AuNPs also possessed oxidase-mimic catalytic performance, which could not only catalyze the oxidation of TMB to blue oxTMB (Fig. 2B), but also catalyze the glucose oxidation (Fig. 2C). When AuNPs were added into the glucose solution for some time, the supernatant would also turn blue after adding TMB and HRP, indicating that H2O2 had been produced after glucose oxidation catalyzed by AuNPs. As the concentration of glucose increased, the absorbance of the solution at 652 nm increased (Fig. 2C). The catalytic kinetics results calculated according to the Lineweaver-Burk plot indicated that (Fig. 2D), the Michaelis-Menten apparent constants (K
m
app ) of AuNPs for glucose was 6.52 mM−1, and the maximum reaction rates (V max) for catalytic oxidation of glucose was 2.18 μM min−1, indicating AuNPs had a high oxidase-mimic catalytic activity for glucose oxidation. Furthermore, when AuNPs were linked to pDNA to form pDNA-Au, the dispersibility was also well (Fig. 2E), and the catalytic performance for glucose oxidation was not significantly decreased compared to that of AuNPs, which K
m
app was 7.10 mM−1, and V max was 2.07 μM min−1 (Fig. 2F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC performances of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 under different conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, p-WO3 doped with 0.5 % ferric ion (denoted as ITO/p-W(Fe)O3) had the best PEC performance compared to other ITO/p-WO3, and its photocurrent response varied under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 3 A, whether in aerobic or anaerobic solutions, the photocurrent response of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 under visible light irradiation was basically similar. This indicated that the presence of oxygen had almost no effect on the photocurrent of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3. In addition, if 10 mM glucose was added into such anaerobic or aerobic solution, the photocurrent of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 all increased significantly under visible light irradiation (Fig. 3A). Adding TMB and HRP into the solution, the absorbance of the solution at 652 nm was not significant (Fig. S4), which indicated that the photocatalytic oxidation of glucose did not produce H2O2. Based on the Mott-Schottky plot of p-W(Fe)O3 and the corresponding potential (Fig. S5), it could be inferred that the conduction band potential (ECB) of p-W(Fe)O3 was +0.64 eV [32], which was relatively positive and the photogenerated electrons on the conduction band were not sufficient to reduce oxygen [33]. Simultaneously, the valence band potential (EVB) of p-W(Fe)O3 was calculated as +3.19 eV according to its Eg of 2.55 eV and ECB of +0.64 eV, where photogenerated holes could directly oxidize glucose without the need for oxygen.

Compared to ITO/p-W(Fe)O3, there was no significant change in the photocurrent of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 after assembling cDNA in anaerobic solution (Fig. 3B). Further bounding with pDNA-Au to form ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/pDNA-Au did not lead to a significant decrease for photocurrent in anaerobic solution (Fig. 3B). However, when such ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/pDNA-Au was immersed in aerobic solution, its photocurrent significantly decreased (Fig. 3B), which should be due to the consumption of photogenerated electrons for oxygen reduction. As mentioned above, the photogenerated electrons on the conduction band of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 could not directly reduce oxygen, so the reduction of oxygen should be caused by the catalytic effect of AuNPs. The photogenerated electrons could be quickly transferred from the conduction band of p-W(Fe)O3 to AuNPs, and then reduce oxygen to the superoxide radicals (·O2 −) with the catalysis of AuNPs, which could be verified by the fluorescence quenching of 2-(2-pyridyliminomethyl) phenol (2-PP) [34]. Fig. S6 showed that the fluorescence intensity of 2-PP decreased significantly due to the production of ·O2 −. Furthermore, the addition of glucose resulted in an instantaneous increase in the photocurrent of the system due to the direct glucose oxidation by photogenerated holes of p-W(Fe)O3 (Fig. 3C). However, as the irradiation time increased, the photocurrent gradually decreased and quickly reached equilibrium in 2 min. This was because the generated ·O2 − was consumed for the glucose oxidation, and AuNPs would need to continuously catalyze the reduction of O2 to produce ·O2 −, thereby continuously consumed photogenerated electrons until equilibrium. When TMB and HRP were added to the above reacted solution, the absorbance at 652 nm significantly increased (Fig. 3D). Therefore, the introduction of AuNPs on ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 led to a decrease in photocurrent of p-W(Fe)O3 and an increase in the absorbance of the reacted solution with adding TMB and HRP, which provided a feasibility for the simultaneous construction of PEC or colorimetric aptasensor for CAP determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the PEC or colorimetric aptasensor for CAP determination
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, the specific construction mechanism of the aptasensor for CAP determination was shown in Scheme 2 . The cDNA was firstly assembled on the surface of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 through amide bonds to form ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA. Then the aptamer was introduced by the complementary base pairing to construct an ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/apt aptasensor. Due to the inability of photogenerated electrons on CB to reduce oxygen, and the photogenerated holes on VB to directly oxidize glucose, ITO/p-W(Fe)O3-cDNA/apt immersed in aerobic solution containing glucose exhibited excellent photocurrent response under visible light irradiation (curve a of Fig. S7A). In the presence of CAP, its aptamer would leave the ITO surface due to its specific binding with CAP. With the catalysis of Exo I, the aptamer with a single-strand structure would be hydrolyzed and CAP could be released and recycled for the next cycle, which was validated by the polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) analysis. The PAGE image showed that (Fig. S7B), compared with the bright band of the CAP-aptamer complex in lane 1, there was nothing left in lane 2 after adding Exo I in CAP-aptamer complex, which indicated that the aptamer binding to CAP had been well hydrolyzed by Exo I into small fragments. With the recycling catalytic hydrolysis by Exo I for the aptamer, more pDNA-Au would hybridize with cDNA and be assembled on the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3. Due to the catalytic performance for oxygen reduction and oxidase-mimic performance of AuNPs for glucose oxidation, the photogenerated electrons would be consumed by oxygen, resulting in a decrease in photocurrent (curve b of Fig. S7A). As a control experiment, if cDNA was directly assembled on the un-activated ITO/p-W(Fe)O3, followed by to assemble the aptamer and pDNA-Au in the presence of CAP, the photocurrent of the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 did not significantly decrease (Fig. S7C), which indicated cDNA could only be effectively assembled through the activated sites on ITO/p-W(Fe)O3, otherwise it would not be possible to determine CAP by the PEC method. In addition, Comparing the results of Fig. S7A and Fig. S7D, blocking non-specific sites on ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 or not had little effect on the final change of photocurrent response, indicating that cDNA was mainly assembled through activated sites on ITO/p-W(Fe)O3, and non-specific sites had little effect on the assembly of cDNA, aptamer, or pDNA-Au. Meanwhile, the absorbance of the above PEC-analyzed solution at 652 nm after adding TMB and HRP also increased in the presence of CAP (Fig. S7E). Therefore, the concentration of CAP in real sample could be determined CAP by PEC or colorimetric methods according to practical need.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performances of the aptasensor for CAP determination
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimized experimental conditions (Fig. S8), the photocurrent of the ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 decreased with the increase of CAP concentration (Fig. 4 A). The logarithm of decrease value of photocurrent (lg△I) had a good linear relationship with the logarithm of CAP concentration (lgC) in the range of 1.0 pM–10.0 nM (Fig. 4B), the fitting linear equation was lg△I = 0.3299 lgC (nM) + 1.7172 (R
2 = 0.9961), and the limit of detection (LOD) of the PEC method was 0.36 pM when the signal-to-noise ratio was 3. Meanwhile, the absorbance of the corresponding PEC-analyzed solution increased with the increase of CAP concentration after adding HRP and TMB (Fig. 4C). The increase value of absorbance at 652 nm (△A) had a good linear relationship with the logarithm of CAP concentration (lgC) in the range of 5.0 pM–10.0 nM (Fig. 4D), the fitted regression equation was △A = 0.1882 lgC (nM) + 0.444 (R
2 = 0.9965), and its LOD of the colorimetric method was 1.72 pM when the signal-to-noise ratio was 3. Compared with other previously reported methods (Table 1 ), the proposed aptasensor exhibited an excellent quantitative range and lower LOD for CAP determination, which mainly attributed to the good PEC response of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3, catalytic activity of AuNPs for glucose oxidation, and Exo I-assisted signal amplification. In addition, the selectivity and stability of the aptasensor for CAP determination by PEC or colorimetric method was investigated. Some antibiotics, such as thiamphenicol (TAP), florfenicol (FF), kanamycin (KAN) and streptomycin sulphate (SS), were selected as potential interferents to study the selectivity of the aptasensor. The results indicated that for PEC determination of CAP, even if their concentrations were 50 times that of CAP (50 nM: 1.0 nM), it had little effect on the CAP determination (Fig. 4E). Similarly, these potential interferents with 50 nM had little effect on determining CAP by the colorimetric method (Fig. S9A), which indicated that the proposed aptasensor had high selectivity for CAP determination. Moreover, the aptasensor had good stability, whether using PEC method or colorimetric method, even after repeated use for 10 weeks, their signal values did not change much (Fig. 4F and Fig. S9B).

To evaluate the application potential of the proposed aptasensor, the concentration of CAP in real lake water and milk samples was determined. Meanwhile, 0.05, 0.50 or 5.00 nM of CAP were added into the samples respectively by the standard addition method to carry out the spiked experiment. The results showed that (Table 2 ), the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 5.7 % for PEC aptasensing and 7.3 % for colorimetric aptasensing, and the recovery rate was in the range of 96.8 %–108.0 % for PEC aptasensing and 90.3–112.0 % for colorimetric aptasensing, which were consistent with those obtained using the standard HPLC method, indicating the good accuracy and precision of the aptasensor for CAP determination whether using PEC or colorimetric aptasensing.

In the present work, we have successfully constructed an aptasensor that can determine CAP through dual signal outputs of PEC and colorimetry. Due to the excellent photocurrent response of ITO/p-W(Fe)O3 and oxidase-mimic catalytic performance of AuNP nanozyme for glucose oxidation, the constructed aptasensor has an ideal quantitative range for determining CAP both through PEC and colorimetric method. Meanwhile, Exo I-assisted recycling catalytic hydrolysis, the measurement signal can be amplified and the constructed sensor has a high sensitivity. Furthermore, the stability of p-W(Fe)O3 and the introduction of aptamer make the aptasensor have good durability and selectivity, thus being applied to real sample analysis with good precision and high accuracy.

Xingyang Wu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Haiyue Wei: Methodology, Investigation. Jiuying Tian: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Jusheng Lu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Top-notch Academic Programs Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (TAPP), the Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation for Higher Education Institutions (19KJB150026), and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Normal University (2024XKT0557, 2022XKT0305) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para:
